There might be a bug here (which was in the existing code as well). If the state update contains the conversation, `_handle_conversation` will add that conversation to the `ConversationList` if it's unknown. However, the conversation in the state update is actually only a delta (see comment in `update_conversation`). This would cause the delta to be used as the complete conversation, and prevent the complete conversation from being fetched.

Since this is a security library care should be taken to isolate trusted content from untrusted content, to ensure that you can actually trust that exception messages don't contain terminal escape characters (see f. ex https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/15101/how-to-avoid-escape-sequence-attacks-in-terminals) or try to include urls or such that a unsuspecting sysadmin might click on. There is a balance to be found here with usability though, good and easy error messages helps a lot when debugging or implementing clients. How about including the unparsed remainder as a property on the exception? A programmer could inspect that where authentication failures are caught fairly easily. Alternatively all non-printable and non-ascii characters could be escaped and still included in the header.

I could go either way on this for the time being, although I do think we ultimately should return a Delorean object in UTC. In my experience, working with datetimes is almost always in UTC, and it would be good to provide a little bit of magic for users here.  We will obviously need to change the documentation.

Part of the draw of using Delorean over datetime should be that it helps inexperienced developers learn how to properly work with datetime objects, and ALWAYS working with UTC unless specified otherwise is a great default to have for n00bs that don't know any better.

NumPy provides a ``lookfor`` function to search APIs. By default ``lookfor`` will search the NumPy API:

I will look into that a little more.  An infinite loop when the logger writes a log message isn't what _should_ be happening.  (I'm not saying it can't happen-- in fact, I think I've seen it.  Only saying it _shouldn't_.)  When the logger writes log messages, they should be going to `STDOUT` and as in the case of all other containers, the `deis-logspout` agent running on the host `deis-logger` runs on should see that message and forward it _to_ `deis-logger`.  `deis-logger` will process that messages (that originated from itself), but it doesn't output (re-output in this case) log messages that is receives... the message should be treated just as is a message from any other component or app.

All that being said, there may be an opportunity to short-circuit that process because it's really inefficient.

So far, when people get stuck on events, it's not because they are confused by the topic filters. We should fix the things we know are broken, and keep transparency to the json-rpc layer where we're not sure.

Some examples of things that I have seen trip people up: 1) default fromBlock value is `latest` and the event they're looking for already happened, 2) and disappearing filters on the client.

Some crazy options for the first:
- deviate from the `fromBlock` default in json-rpc, so that it is 0
- *require* that people specify a `fromBlock` when creating a filter?
- always return a result with log queries, with a value like `{fromBlock: 1234, toBlock:1237, events: [], allEvents: [{event-that-did-not-match}]}`, so that it's clear what blocks are being inspected. There are negative performance implications, but maybe you could opt out of that behavior for production.

Why does this affect flow control and not `request_byte_threshold`? The 5MB limit is for a single request (i.e. a single bundle), which is what `request_byte_threshold` will affect. The flow control setting will affect the total number of bytes that can be queued in the bundler before additional calls either block or throw an exception. So the `request_byte_threshold` could be 4MB and the flow control setting could be 40MB, which would mean a max of approx 10 max size bundles could be queued.

I have one more v2 change coming down the line. In my opinion, the `Client` class won't be dramatically changed by it; there will be two new public methods, and one new private method.

> What happens if you call new_order on a client for ACMEv1 or request_challenges on a client for ACMEv2?

For new_order on an ACMEv1 client, we can pretty easily do the right thing: Turn it into a set of request_challenges. For request_challenges on ACMEv2 we could arguably synthesize an order out of them (now that you don't need a CSR to create an order), but it is probably better to just blow up.

In my opinion, on balance, the changes are not obtrusive enough to justify a whole new class.

Possessive case in English. A form of the genitive case used in a genitive-noun relationship.

You put the `'s` at the end to indicate posession. In our case to indicate that the deposit belongs to the partner.
There are some exceptions to this rule but they don't apply here.

You could maintain the same semantics by using genitive with composition and turn it into:

> with up to the deposit **of** the partner

The problem is opening a driver while it's already open.
If you try opening the driver again before closing it will return 0 for all get functions.
```
import this
that = this.driver()
that.illegal_call()   #could be non existing methods or illegal values caught by a validator
>>> Exception #this stops the script from executing further commands

#we run the program again:
that = this.driver() #from here on the driver will only return 0 for any get function
```
The driver can be returned to a usable state by doing one of the following:

- close the driver and run the script again
- close the ipython console and run the script again

As long as your script that opens and closes the driver doesn't give an error everything works properly.
